elements.sent-2411-001: Supercontinuum (SC) generation arises from the intricate interplay of third-order and higher-order nonlinear optical processes when an ultrashort pulse propagates through a dispersive medium.
elements.sent-2411-002: Femtosecond (fs) laser oscillators delivering pulses with energies in the nanojoule (nJ) range can drive SC generation in highly nonlinear media such as photonic-crystal fibers or bulk-glass fibers \cite{Ranka2000OL,agrawal2000nonlinear,alfano2006supercontinuum,dudley2006supercontinuum,Sell2009OE .}
elements.sent-2411-003: The resulting SC significantly broadens the input pulse’s spectrum, creating a continuous, octave-spanning spectral band.
elements.sent-2411-004: With high brightness and exceptional spectral coverage, SC sources have become indispensable resources for applications including spectroscopy \cite{borondics2018supercontinuum , advanced microscopy \cite{kaminski2008supercontinuum,gundougdu2023self , and optical coherence tomography \cite{tu2013coherent,ji2021millimeter .}}}
elements.sent-2411-005: In ultrafast photonics, SC serves as a cornerstone for breakthroughs in ultrashort laser sources, including self-referenced \cite{Jones2000Sci and offset-free \cite{Sinclair2015comb,Fehrenbacher2015Optica frequency combs, generation of few- and single-cycle pulses \cite{Krauss2010NatPhot,shumakova2016multi,steinleitner2022single , and high-harmonic generation as well as attosecond pulse \cite{peng2019attosecond,li2020attosecond .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-006: The precise manipulation of supercontinuum (SC) spectra is highly desirable but poses significant experimental challenges.
elements.sent-2411-007: This difficulty arises from the extreme sensitivity of SC generation to high-order dispersion and input pulse parameters, which drive a cascade of nonlinear interactions, including self- and cross-phase modulation (SPM and XPM), four-wave mixing (FWM), and Raman scattering.
elements.sent-2411-008: Commanding these processes to produce predictable SC outputs is formidable, requiring detailed characterization of linear dispersion, input pulse parameters, material dimensions, and third-order nonlinear coefficients \cite{alfano2006supercontinuum, Sell2009OE, bres2023supercontinuum .}
elements.sent-2411-009: Achieving such control could enable the routine generation of octave-spanning and other synthesized spectra, thereby democratizing access to \Blue{few-cycle waveform shaping \cite{Krauss2010NatPhot, steinleitner2022single .}}
elements.sent-2411-010: Various strategies have been developed to gain control over SC generation.
elements.sent-2411-011: One approach involves selecting materials or waveguides with favorable dispersion properties \cite{Sell2009OE or tailoring the nonlinearity of the medium, for instance, by adjusting the pressure inside gas-filled hollow-core fibers \cite{beetar2020multioctave, elu2021seven, piccoli2021intense .}}
elements.sent-2411-012: Other methods focus on modifying the input pulse characteristics, such as pulse duration \cite{genty2007fiber , energy \cite{genier2021ultra , and pulse number \cite{wetzel2018customizing , to tune the SC spectral properties \cite{dudley2006supercontinuum, sylvestre2021recent .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Incorporating optimization algorithms, particularly genetic algorithms (GA), has enabled dynamic control of SC features \cite{tzang2018adaptive, wetzel2018customizing, hary2023tailored, lapre2023genetic , though the iterative search process, typically takes several minutes.}
elements.sent-2411-014: The main advantage of machine learning algorithms, such as artificial neural networks (ANNs), is to reduce this search time dramatically, from minutes to mere seconds, by mapping the highly nonlinear system onto a regression task \cite{farfan2018femtosecond, boscolo2020artificial, salmela2022feed .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Experimental investigations have also leveraged machine learning to analyze specific SC properties, particularly in identifying extreme events \cite{narhi2018machine, salmela2020machine .}
elements.sent-2411-016: These studies typically rely on data-driven models \cite{genty2021machine , using phase or intensity inputs to train neural networks.}
elements.sent-2411-017: Despite these advancements, SC generation presents a strongly under-determined problem due to the dramatic spectral broadening relative to the input pulse.
elements.sent-2411-018: For example, the spectral-temporal features of SC are highly sensitive to the input wave's spectral phase, which evolves intricately during nonlinear propagation.
elements.sent-2411-019: When small perturbations become comparable to the experimental noise (numerical instabilities) in the training datasets, the search for the true solution becomes compromised.
elements.sent-2411-020: Consequently, large and accurate training datasets are often required for adequate network convergence \cite{boscolo2020artificial,genty2021machine .}
elements.sent-2411-021: In this work, we introduce a physics-trained convolutional neural network (P-CNN) to model pulse propagation in highly nonlinear media.
elements.sent-2411-022: The Wigner function represents wavepackets in the spectral and temporal (chronocyclic) domain, enabling the use of convolutional neural networks (CNNs) to efficiently filter out uncorrelated noise and thus to reduce sensitivity to phase variations and eliminate artifacts related to the carrier-envelope offset phase (CEP) \cite{zuo2022deep .}
elements.sent-2411-023: Combined with a real-time optimization strategy, the trained P-CNN allows precise control of spectral and temporal dynamics, potentially down to single-cycle pulses.
elements.sent-2411-024: Experimentally, we validated the P-CNN’s performance across a broad range of input conditions.
elements.sent-2411-025: For strong input pulses, we demonstrated on-demand control of SC generation, including precise tuning of the bandwidth and spectral position.
elements.sent-2411-026: In the temporal domain, we achieved pulse durations as short as 12 fs (2–3 optical cycles) with a nonlinear compression factor of over 70, eliminating the need for expensive pulse compression methods \cite{dudley2006supercontinuum, piccoli2021intense .}
elements.sent-2411-027: For weaker input pulses, we showed the ability to generate high-order solitons and performed detailed spectro-temporal analyses of their dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-028: These results underscore the P-CNN’s capability to advance spectro-temporal manipulation of SC generation.
elements.section-2411-002: P-CNN framework via the nonlinear optical platform
elements.sent-2411-029: Figure \ref{F1 provides a comprehensive overview of the SC process and the architecture of the P-CNN.}
elements.sent-2411-030: The optical setup consists of three primary sections.
elements.sent-2411-031: The first section, responsible for dispersion engineering (DE), includes a mode-locked fiber laser that generates femtosecond soliton pulses and a 4$f$ pulse shaper, which enables precise control of dispersion.
elements.sent-2411-032: The output of the DE section feeds into the nonlinear engineering (NE) section, which comprises an Erbium-doped fiber amplifier (EDFA) and a highly nonlinear fiber (HNF) for supercontinuum generation.
elements.sent-2411-033: This setup allows for octave-spanning spectral broadening, unlocking advanced manipulation of SC outputs.
elements.sent-2411-034: For further details about the setup and simulations of SC generation, refer to \hyperref[sec:methods1]{Methods-1: Setup and Simulations for Supercontinuum Generation .}
elements.sent-2411-035: The dynamics of a laser pulse with a temporal envelope $A$ propagating through the described setup can be modeled by a revised Generalized Nonlinear Schrödinger Equation (GNLSE) \cite{dudley2006supercontinuum : \begin{equation}\label{E-1} \frac{{\partial A}}{{\partial z}} + \frac{\alpha }{2}A +\left[\sum_{\alpha=0}^2\frac{iD_\alpha}{2}\left(\frac{-\partial^2}{\partial t^2}\right)^{\frac{\alpha}{2}} -\sum\limits_{k \geq 2} \frac{{i^{k + 1}{\beta _k}}}{{k!}} \frac{{\partial ^k}}{{\partial {t^k}}} \right]A= i\gamma \left( {1 + i{\tau _{shock}}\frac{\partial }{{\partial t}}} \right)\left( {A \int_{ - \infty }^{ + \infty } R(t') \left| {A(z,t - t')} \right|^2 dt' + i{\Gamma _R}(z,t)} \right).\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}\label{E-1} \frac{{\partial A}}{{\partial z}} + \frac{\alpha }{2}A +\left[\sum_{\alpha=0}^2\frac{iD_\alpha}{2}\left(\frac{-\partial^2}{\partial t^2}\right)^{\frac{\alpha}{2}} -\sum\limits_{k \geq 2} \frac{{i^{k + 1}{\beta _k}}}{{k!}} \frac{{\partial ^k}}{{\partial {t^k}}} \right]A= i\gamma \left( {1 + i{\tau _{shock}}\frac{\partial }{{\partial t}}} \right)\left( {A \int_{ - \infty }^{ + \infty } R(t') \left| {A(z,t - t')} \right|^2 dt' + i{\Gamma _R}(z,t)} \right).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-036: Here, the left-hand side of the equation models linear propagation effects, in which the third term represents fractional derivatives, while the fourth term describes regular higher-order derivatives.
elements.sent-2411-037: Both can be independently controlled using the 4$f$ pulse shaper, where the fractional derivative introduces fractional dispersion (see, e.g., Refs.. \citenum{malomed2021optical,liu2023experimental ).}
elements.sent-2411-038: The right-hand side accounts for nonlinear effects.
elements.sent-2411-039: The Kerr coefficient $\gamma$ governs the nonlinear refractive index, while $R(t)$ is the Raman response function.
elements.sent-2411-040: The term $\Gamma_R(z,t)$ captures the influence of spontaneous Raman noise.
elements.sent-2411-041: Additional nonlinear phenomena, such as self-steepening and optical shock formation, are characterized by a timescale $\tau_{shock = \tau_0 = 1/\omega_0$.}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=17cm]{Fig_Setup_AI_S4.png} \caption{Experimental platform for spectro-temporal engineering assisted by a physics-trained convolutional neural network (P-CNN). (a) Schematized setup includes dispersion engineering, nonlinear engineering, and the supercontinuum generation stages. (b) The network architecture of P-CNN features the physical layer and CNN layer. Visualized spectro-temporal space of P-CNN output (right). (c)-(d) Regression comparison for the trained P-CNN and Artificial Neural Network (ANN). }\label{F1}
elements.sent-2411-042: Typically, the dynamics of SC generation are resolved through numerical simulations based on Eq.~\ref{E-1 .}
elements.sent-2411-043: However, such simulations are computationally intensive and highly sensitive to various parameters, including the energy and spectro-temporal details of the realistic input pulse shape $A_{in (t)$, which are often derived experimentally \cite{Sell2009OE .}}
elements.sent-2411-044: Machine learning algorithms present a promising alternative by simplifying this complex nonlinear system into a manageable linear representation.
elements.sent-2411-045: A convolutional neural network (CNN) can efficiently map the initial input field to the desired SC output parameters, expressed as: \begin{equation}\label{E-2} I(Y) = \mathbb{P-CNN}_{\theta}[\Phi(X)],\end{equation} where $\Phi(X)$ represents the spectral phase of the input field, and $I(Y)$ corresponds to the output parameters, such as spectral intensity $I(\omega)$ or temporal intensity $I(t)$.
elements.sent-2411-046: The P-CNN thus establishes a mapping between these parameter spaces via a nonlinear transformation $\theta$, as illustrated in Fig.~\ref{F1 (b).}
elements.sent-2411-047: The success of this mapping hinges on the CNN's ability to robustly identify optimal solutions despite the SC process’s extreme sensitivity to input conditions.
elements.sent-2411-048: The $\mathbb{P-CNN}$ framework integrates physical principles into a CNN, enabling robust determination of $\theta$.
elements.sent-2411-049: To address the sensitivity of direct training on such profiles \cite{zuo2022deep , we reconstruct the pulse field $A_{in (t)$ using complex spectral amplitude data retrieved from a Second-Harmonic-Generation Frequency-Resolved Optical Gating (SHG-FROG) setup.}}
elements.sent-2411-050: The Wigner function, which represents the pulse in the chronocyclic domain \cite{walmsley2009characterization , is then calculated: \begin{equation}\label{E-3} W(t,\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} A\left(t + \frac{t'}{2}\right) A^*\left(t - \frac{t'}{2}\right) \exp{(i\omega t')} dt'.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-051: This transformation ensures a self-consistent representation of the pulse in both the time and frequency domains, as depicted in the `Physical Layer' of Fig.~\ref{F1 (b).}
elements.sent-2411-052: The Wigner function is then fed into the CNN for training, with further details provided in \hyperref[sec:methods2]{Methods-2: The Structure and Performance of Deep Learning Neural Networks .}
elements.sent-2411-053: The physics-trained network not only embeds spectro-temporal correlations into the model but also transforms the nonlinear SC generation problem into a network presentation.
elements.sent-2411-054: This enables the efficient identification of inverse solutions for the desired SC state.
elements.sent-2411-055: We validated the performance of the trained P-CNN through simulations based on the GNLSE described in Eq.~\ref{E-1 .}
elements.sent-2411-056: Figure~\ref{F1 (c) shows a linear regression analysis comparing the target SC, yielding a correlation factor $R = 0.97$ for the fitting line.}
elements.sent-2411-057: For comparison, using the same input data, we trained an artificial neural network (ANN) with three hidden layers containing 200, 200, and 100 neurons, respectively, commonly used for SC simulations \cite{boscolo2020artificial, salmela2022feed .}
elements.sent-2411-058: The regression results, shown in Fig.~\ref{F1 (d), yield a correlation factor of $R \approx 0.91$.}
elements.sent-2411-059: By interpreting $(1-R)$ as the fraction of uncompensated spectral correlations, the P-CNN demonstrates superior performance with a threefold reduction in sensitivity to nonlinear phase variations compared to the ANN.
elements.sent-2411-060: This improvement underscores the robustness of the physics-trained deep learning framework, forecasting significant speedups in achieving various target functions.
elements.sent-2411-061: These networks are designed to handle different pulse energy regimes.
elements.sent-2411-062: For pulse energies $\lessapprox$ 2 nJ, the EDFA facilitates nonlinear spectral broadening through SPM, as shown in Fig.~\ref{F1 (a).}
elements.sent-2411-063: This regime operates in the anomalous dispersion domain, leading to spectral sidelobe generation and soliton formation.
elements.sent-2411-064: For higher pulse energies, a cascade of EDFA and HNF generates SC characterized by an ultra-broadband dispersive wave, blueshifted into the normal dispersion regime.

elements.sent-2411-065: Both simulations and experiments indicate that the trained P-CNN can efficiently optimize $\Phi(\omega)$ to achieve virtually any physical target function.
elements.sent-2411-066: To further enhance performance, a real-time gradient descent search algorithm was implemented within the optical fiber system, utilizing the optimized solutions (see \hyperref[sec:sup2]{Supplementary - 2 for details).}
elements.sent-2411-067: We first examine the controlled energy transfer from the soliton region (Net-S) to the dispersive wave (DW) region (Net-D).
elements.sent-2411-068: The Net-D region spans 1000–1430 nm, while the Net-S region covers 1430–1700 nm, constrained by the optical spectrum analyzer’s (OSA) range.
elements.sent-2411-069: Figure~\ref{F2 (a) shows the measured dependence of the SC output on the input spectral phase in log-linear scaling.}
elements.sent-2411-070: Starting with modest broadening dominated by SPM (curve 1), P-CNN-assisted phase optimization progressively redistributes energy from soliton to dispersive wave (curves 4, 9, and 15).
elements.sent-2411-071: This process is quantified using the nonlinear conversion efficiency (NCE): \begin{equation}\label{E_R} \eta_{NCE} = \frac{S_d}{S_d + S_s} \in [0, 1],\end{equation} where $S_{d,s = \int_{d,s I(\lambda) d\lambda$ represents the integrated spectral energy over the respective spectral regions.}}
elements.sent-2411-072: An $\eta_{NCE}$ of 1 indicates complete energy transfer to dispersion wave, while 0 signifies no transfer.
elements.sent-2411-073: Figure~\ref{F2 (b) depicts the measured NCE, showing linear growth followed by oscillations, with a maximum conversion of 12\%.}
elements.sent-2411-074: In the high-NCE, we examine the DW centered around $\sim$1070 nm, which forms due to nonlinear interactions in the normal dispersion regime.
elements.sent-2411-075: Next, we demonstrate the P-CNN's ability to achieve several features of DW, focusing on the center wavelength and bandwidth.
elements.sent-2411-076: The central wavelength, $\lambda_{DS}$, is defined by: \begin{equation}\label{E_creep} \lambda_{DS} = \lambda_0 + \Delta\lambda\cos(\Theta),\end{equation} where $\lambda_0$ is the baseline wavelength, $\Delta\lambda$ is the modulation amplitude, and $\Theta$ is the angular coordinate.
elements.sent-2411-077: Figure~\ref{F2 (c) shows excellent agreement between the target modulation (solid blue line, $\Delta\lambda$ = 20 nm, $\lambda_0$ = 1070 nm) and measured data, achieving a 3.7\% modulation of the DW center wavelength.}
elements.sent-2411-078: We also demonstrate bandwidth manipulation with the DW center wavelength clamped at 1070 nm.
elements.sent-2411-079: The measured bandwidth closely follows the target range of 60-100 nm, as shown in Fig.~\ref{F2 (d).}
elements.sent-2411-080: These combined results demonstrate the feasibility of a full engineering of the spectral density of the DW, while also tracing out a path toward an elegant control over the DW few-cycle temporal wavepacket.

elements.sent-2411-102: In this section, we demonstrate the capability of the P-CNN to perform nonlinear pulse shaping in both the spectral and temporal domains.

elements.sent-2411-110: Next, we explore the feasibility of generation of high-order solitons within the current setup.
elements.sent-2411-111: High-order solitons are a cornerstone of nonlinear optics, characterized by their unique dynamics during propagation.
elements.sent-2411-112: To define the target function,we initiate the pulse as a high-order soliton: \( A(t) = N \, \text{sech (t) \), and then simulate its dynamics during propagation using the NLSE.}
elements.sent-2411-113: During propagation along \( z \), spetro-temporal breather phenomena arise due to the imbalance between the dispersion and SPM for such high-order soliton.
elements.sent-2411-114: The soliton period $z_0$, which dictates the propagation length, is evaluated by: \begin{equation}z_0 = \frac{\pi}{2} L_d,\end{equation} where \( L_d = T_0^2 / |\beta_2| \) is the dispersion length.
elements.sent-2411-115: Specifically, the spectral evolution of a third-order soliton ($N$ = 3) over one soliton period is illustrated in Fig. \ref{F4 (b).}
elements.sent-2411-116: In the time domain, the soliton contracts to a fraction of its initial width, splits into two pulses at \( z_0/2 \), and then recombines to its original form at \( z = z_0 \).
elements.sent-2411-117: This breathing pattern recurs over successive propagation segments \( z \).
elements.sent-2411-118: Practically, capturing the breather pattern would require physically cutting the fiber, an irreversible and labor-intensive process.

elements.sent-2411-129: A fundamental model can elucidate the observed dynamics in high-order soliton generation.
elements.sent-2411-130: By analyzing the optimized spectral phase of the initial pulse, reconstructed using a FROG system, we derived the temporal intensity and extracted key parameters, including pulse duration (\(T_0\)) and peak power (\(P_k\)).
elements.sent-2411-131: From these, we calculated the dispersion length (\(L_d=T_0^2/|\beta_2|\)) and nonlinear length (\(L_n = 1/(\gamma P_k)\)).
elements.sent-2411-132: These parameters define the soliton number: \begin{equation}N^2 = \frac{L_d}{L_n} \approx T_0^2 P_k.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-133: Maintaining a consistent soliton number for each sequence is critical for accurately reproducing high-order soliton profiles at the fiber output.
elements.sent-2411-134: Any deviation can destabilize soliton dynamics \cite{agrawal2000nonlinear,malomed2006soliton .}

elements.sent-2411-143: In this work, we introduced a physics-trained deep learning framework based on a convolutional neural network, termed P-CNN, designed for flexible spectro-temporal engineering of photonic wavepackets.
elements.sent-2411-144: Unlike the physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) developed in recent years, which are effective for solving partial differential equations (PDEs) and self-learning machines \cite{karniadakis2021physics,lopez2023self , our approach incorporates the physical representation directly into the input layer rather than the loss function.}
elements.sent-2411-145: This distinction allows for a more intrinsic integration of physics-based constraints into the network.
elements.sent-2411-146: This framework was implemented using an active pulse shaper paired with a nonlinear broadening section consisting of an EDFA and a highly nonlinear fiber assembly.
elements.sent-2411-147: Output pulse characterization via SH-FROG enabled wavepacket-based training of the P-CNN.

elements.sent-2411-151: For Net-D, we demonstrated fine control over DW properties, including center frequency, bandwidth, and pulse duration.

elements.sent-2411-155: Regarding Net-S, we demonstrated on-demand generation of high-order solitons ($N = 3, 4$), capturing characteristic breather dynamics in the spectro-temporal domain as predicted by the nonlinear Schrödinger equation.
elements.sent-2411-156: These demonstrations highlight the remarkable capabilities of P-CNN-enabled pulse manipulation across both spectral and temporal domains.

elements.sent-2411-169: It arises from the intricate interplay of third-order and higher-order nonlinear optical processes when the fs pulse propagates through a dispersive medium.
elements.sent-2411-170: Femtosecond laser oscillators delivering pulses with energies in the nanojoule (nJ) range can drive SC generation in highly nonlinear media such as photonic-crystal fibers or bulk glass \cite{Ranka2000OL,agrawal2000nonlinear,alfano2006supercontinuum,dudley2006supercontinuum,Sell2009OE .}
elements.sent-2411-171: The resulting SC significantly broadens the incident pulse spectrum, creating a continuous, octave-spanning spectral band.
elements.sent-2411-172: With high brightness and exceptional spectral coverage, SC has become an indispensable resource for applications such as spectroscopy \cite{borondics2018supercontinuum , advanced microscopy \cite{kaminski2008supercontinuum,gundougdu2023self , and optical coherence tomography \cite{tu2013coherent,ji2021millimeter .}}}
elements.sent-2411-173: In ultrafast photonics, SC generation and precise control of their spectro-temporal features serves as a cornerstone for breakthroughs in ultrashort laser sources, including self-referenced \cite{Jones2000Sci,Sinclair2015comb frequency combs \cite{Fehrenbacher2015Optica , few- and single-cycle pulses \cite{Krauss2010NatPhot,shumakova2016multi,steinleitner2022single , and high-harmonic generation as well as attosecond pulse \cite{Lewenstein1994PRA,Corkum2007NatPhys,Krausz2024RMP,peng2019attosecond,li2020attosecond .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-174: Although the precise manipulation of SC spectra is highly desirable, it poses significant experimental challenges.
elements.sent-2411-175: The essential difficulty arises from the extreme sensitivity of SC generation to complex dispersion and input pulse parameters, which drive a cascade of nonlinear interactions, including self- and cross-phase modulation (SPM and XPM), four-wave mixing (FWM), and Raman scattering.
elements.sent-2411-176: The task of directing these processes to produce predictable SC outputs is formidable, which requires detailed characterization of the parameters of the materials, such as dispersion, third-order nonlinearity \cite{alfano2006supercontinuum, Sell2009OE, bres2023supercontinuum , and input temporal profile \cite{lozovoy2004multiphoton,xu2006quantitative,monmayrant2010newcomer .}}
elements.sent-2411-177: Achieving such control could enable the routine generation of octave-spanning, thereby democratizing access to few-cycle pulse shaping \cite{Krauss2010NatPhot, steinleitner2022single .}
elements.sent-2411-178: Various strategies have been developed to control over SC generation.
elements.sent-2411-179: One approach involves selecting materials or waveguides with favorable dispersion properties \cite{Sell2009OE,Carlson2017OL or tailoring the non-linearity of the medium, for example, by adjusting the pressure inside gas-filled hollow core fibers \cite{beetar2020multioctave, elu2021seven, piccoli2021intense .}}
elements.sent-2411-180: Other methods focus on modifying the input pulse specifications, such as pulse duration \cite{genty2007fiber , energy \cite{genier2021ultra , and pulse number \cite{wetzel2018customizing , to adjust SC spectral properties \cite{dudley2006supercontinuum, sylvestre2021recent .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-181: Incorporating optimization algorithms, particularly genetic algorithms (GA) in an iterative process, has enabled dynamic control of SC features \cite{tzang2018adaptive, wetzel2018customizing, hary2023tailored, lapre2023genetic , in which the searching time typically takes several minutes.}
elements.sent-2411-182: Recent theoretical studies employing machine learning have shown promising capabilities in predicting the spectral or temporal characteristics of SC \cite{martins2022design, hoang2022optimizing, shih2023maximizing .}
elements.sent-2411-183: The main feature of machine learning algorithms, such as feed-forward neural networks (FNNs), is the ability to learn the pulse evolution in the nonlinear complex systems, dramatically reducing the time toward optimized target functions from minutes to mere seconds \cite{farfan2018femtosecond, boscolo2020artificial, salmela2022feed .}
elements.sent-2411-184: By leveraging machine learning, experimental investigations have analyzed specific SC properties, particularly identifying extreme events \cite{narhi2018machine, salmela2020machine .}
elements.sent-2411-185: These studies typically rely on data-driven models \cite{genty2021machine , using phase and intensity inputs to train a neural network.}
elements.sent-2411-186: Despite these advances, SC generation presents a strongly underdetermined problem due to the dramatic spectral broadening relative to the input pulse.
elements.sent-2411-187: The spectro-temporal correlations of SC are highly sensitive to the input wave's spectral phase and amplitude, which evolves intricately during nonlinear propagation.
elements.sent-2411-188: When small correlations become comparable to the experimental noise (or numerical instabilities) in the training datasets, the search for the optimized solution becomes compromised.
elements.sent-2411-189: In this work, we introduce a \Red{physics-embedded convolutional neural network (P-CNN) to address these challenges and perform spectro-temporal pulse shaping in nonlinear optics.}
elements.sent-2411-190: We use the term "\Red{physics-embedded " to underscore the fact that we embed the physical transform, i.e., spectro-temporal correlation function, specifically the Wigner function, into the front of network instead of relying solely on data-driven models.}
elements.sent-2411-191: The Wigner function represents wavepackets in the spectral and temporal (chronocyclic) domain, enabling the CNNs to filter out uncorrelated noise efficiently, thus reducing sensitivity to phase variations such as carrier-envelope offset phase (CEP) \cite{zuo2022deep .}
elements.sent-2411-192: Furthermore, additional simulations demonstrate that even a simple FNN —when embedded with Wigner-function-based input (referred to as P-FNN)— can also achieve significantly improved performance.
elements.sent-2411-193: This underscores the importance of physics-embedded input representations, regardless of model complexity.
elements.sent-2411-194: Experimentally, we demonstrate a high nonlinear optical platform with implementation of two P-CNNs.
elements.sent-2411-195: Enabled by one P-CNN, the system shows continuous tuning of SC's spectral and temporal features, including central frequency, bandwidth, and pulse duration.
elements.sent-2411-196: Notably, we achieve 12 fs pulses (sub-three optical cycle; a 70-fold nonlinear compression of pulse duration) directly out of a SC fiber, thus eliminating the need for an external pulse compressor \cite{dudley2006supercontinuum, piccoli2021intense .}
elements.sent-2411-197: Using the second P-CNN, the system can directly output high-order soliton waveform over arbitrary spectral-temporal profile.
elements.sent-2411-198: These results underscore the physics-embedded network advancements in manipulation of SC, paving the way for precise and efficient control over spectro-temporal laser state in highly nonlinear system.
elements.sent-2411-199: Figure \ref{F1 (a) provides an overview of our nonlinear optical platform for implementation of P-CNN in the context of the SC generation.}
elements.sent-2411-200: The setup consists of four primary sections.
elements.sent-2411-201: The first section, responsible for dispersion engineering, includes a mode-locked fiber laser that generates femtosecond soliton pulses and a 4$f$ pulse shaper, which enables precise complex dispersion control.
elements.sent-2411-202: The output pulse is fed into the second nonlinear engineering section, which comprises an Erbium-doped fiber amplifier (EDFA) to address pulse energy enhancement and self-phase modulation(SPM).
elements.sent-2411-203: The third section is a highly nonlinear fiber (HNF), which is used for supercontinuum (SC) generation.
elements.sent-2411-204: The spectrum (or temporal features) of SC is measured in the last section, using a pulse characterization setup.
elements.sent-2411-205: This platform allows for engineering of an octave-spanning spectral broadening of SC, unlocking advanced manipulation of few-cycle pulse shaping.

elements.sent-2411-206: The dynamics of a laser pulse with a temporal envelope $A(z,t)$ propagating through a HNF can be modeled by a Generalized Nonlinear Schrödinger Equation (GNLSE) \cite{dudley2006supercontinuum : \begin{equation}\label{E-1} \qty[\pdv{z}-i\sum_{k\geq2}\frac{i^k\beta _k}{k!}\pdv[k]{t}+\frac\alpha2]A(z,t)=\gamma\qty(i-\tau_{\mathrm{shock}}\pdv{t})\qty[i\Gamma_R(z,t)+A(z,t)\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}dt'\;R(t')\abs{A(z,t-t')}^2].\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-207: Here, $z$ is the propagation coordinate and $t$ represents the retarded time $t=T-\beta_1z$ evolving with the group velocity (first-order dispersion) $\beta_1$ of the envelope.
elements.sent-2411-208: The left-hand side of the equation models linear propagation effects.
elements.sent-2411-209: The right-hand side accounts for nonlinear effects, in which the Kerr coefficient $\gamma$ governs the nonlinear refractive index, and $R(t)$ is the Raman response function.
elements.sent-2411-210: Typically, the dynamics of SC generation is resolved through numerical simulations based on Eq.~\ref{E-1 .}
elements.sent-2411-211: However, such simulations are computationally intensive and highly sensitive to the aforementioned parameters and the shape of the input pulse \(A_\text{in (t)\), which are often derived experimentally \cite{Sell2009OE .}}
elements.sent-2411-212: Each initial condition defines a unique SC output through a nonlinear mapping process.
elements.sent-2411-213: Since the input temporal field is generally defined in the spectral domain via the Fourier transform \(\mathcal{F}\), it can be expressed as \({A _{\text{in (t) = \mathcal{F ^{-1}\left[\tilde A_{\text{in (\omega)\exp\left(i\Phi(\omega)\right)\right]\), where \(\tilde A_{\text{in (\omega)\) and \(\Phi(\omega)\) denote the spectral amplitude and phase, respectively.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-214: Thus, direct observation of the spectral or temporal intensity \(\text{I[t ,\omega\text{]}\) of the SC provides experimental access to the targeted parameter space of \(\Phi(\omega)\).}
elements.sent-2411-215: A robust program for engineering SC is to find a nonlinear mapping of an angle \(\theta\) between the parameter space and the observed output \(\text{I[t ,\omega\text{]}\), as sketched in Fig.~\ref{F1 (b).}}
elements.sent-2411-216: Finding the accurate angle \(\theta\) can be quite challenging both in simulations of Eq.~\ref{E-1 and in experiments, due to the interplay of nonlinear processes and corresponding sensitivity to experimental (or computational) noise.}
elements.sent-2411-217: Machine learning algorithms present a promising solution by simplifying this complex nonlinear system into a manageable network representation.
elements.sent-2411-218: As shown in Fig.~\ref{F1 (c), a \Red{physics-embedded convolutional neural network (P-CNN) is introduced to efficiently learn the nonlinear mapping, expressed as: \begin{equation}\label{E-2} \text{I[t},\omega\text{]}=\text{P-CNN}_{\rm{\theta}}[\Phi(\omega)]=\rm{CNN}[\mathcal{T}(\Phi(\omega))]=\rm{CNN}[W(t,\omega)],\end{equation} where the P-CNN is based on the CNN embedded with the physical transform \(\mathcal{T (\Phi(\omega))\).}}}
elements.sent-2411-219: The physical transform, here the Wigner function, is designed to represent the pulse in the chronocyclic domain\cite{walmsley2009characterization,najafabadi2024intensity , containing the structure of spectro-temporal correlations: \begin{equation}\label{E-3} W(t,\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}dt'\; A\qty(t + \frac{t'}{2}) A^*\qty(t - \frac{t'}{2})\,e^{i\omega t'}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-220: Here $A(t)$ is associated with the spectral amplitude $\tilde A(\omega)$ and phase $\Phi(\omega)$.
elements.sent-2411-221: Experimentally, such pulse profile can be reconstructed by using time-dependent spectrograms, such as Second-Harmonic-Generation Frequency-Resolved Optical Gating (SHG-FROG).
elements.sent-2411-222: The success of the mapping (Eq. \ref{E-2 ) hinges on CNN's ability to robustly identify the nonlinear transformation $\theta$, and thus find the optimal inverse solutions for SC engineering, with further details provided in \hyperref[sec:methods2]{Methods-2: The Structure and Performance of Deep Learning Neural Networks .}}

elements.sent-2411-278: Next, we explore the feasibility to directly generate high-order solitons within the current setup.
elements.sent-2411-279: High-order solitons (typically labeled by a soliton number $N$) are a cornerstone of nonlinear optics, characterized by their unique dynamics during propagation \cite{agrawal2000nonlinear .}
elements.sent-2411-280: When a high-energy soliton pulse, \( A(t) = N \, \text{sech (t) \), where $N$ is an integer soliton number, is seeded into a nonlinear fiber, spectro-temporal breather phenomena arise due to the imbalance between dispersion and self-phase modulation (SPM) during propagation along \( z \).}

elements.sent-2411-284: In the following, we present the results observed from the P-CNN and also explain them using the theoretical model of high-order solitons.

elements.sent-2411-286: How do we prove that the dynamics simulated by P-CNN is of the solitonic kind? From the spectral phase and amplitude of the initial pulse optimized by the network and real system, we extract the temporal intensity, pulse duration (\(T_0\)) and peak power (\(P_k\)).
elements.sent-2411-287: Using these, we check the dispersion length (\(L_d=T_0^2/|\beta_2|\)) and nonlinear length (\(L_n = 1/(\gamma P_k)\)), which define the soliton number: \begin{equation}\label{E-soliton} N^2 = \frac{L_d}{L_n} \approx T_0^2 P_k.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-288: Eq. \ref{E-soliton emphasizes that the physical system must have the ability to control both the pulse duration \(T_0\) and the peak power \(P_k\), as these parameters directly influence the dispersion and nonlinear lengths.}

elements.sent-2411-293: In this work, we introduced a \Red{physics-embedded deep learning framework based on a convolutional neural network (CNN), termed P-CNN, designed for versatile spectro-temporal engineering of photonic wavepackets.}
elements.sent-2411-294: This physics-embedded input structure boosts both CNN and FNN models to learn physically grounded features that capture the underlying ultrafast dynamics, leading to significantly lower prediction errors and improved robustness to noise compared to purely data-driven models.
elements.sent-2411-295: The framework was implemented and validated experimentally using an active pulse shaper combined with a highly nonlinear broadening section.
elements.sent-2411-296: The characterization of the output pulse via SHG-FROG facilitated wavepacket-based training data for the P-CNN.
elements.sent-2411-297: By analyzing the generated supercontinuum using two distinct networks, Net-D and Net-S, we successfully modeled the dynamics of dispersive waves (DW) and soliton waves (SW), respectively.
elements.sent-2411-298: For Net-D, we achieved precise control over the DW properties, including center frequency, bandwidth, and pulse duration.
elements.sent-2411-299: Remarkably, a minimum infrared pulse duration of 12 fs—corresponding to sub-three optical cycles and near-transform-limited performance was achieved without external pulse compressors, enabled by the P-CNN’s ability to balance nonlinear broadening and pulse compression.
elements.sent-2411-300: For Net-S, we demonstrated on-demand generation of high-order solitons ($N = 3, 4$), capturing characteristic breather dynamics in the spectro-temporal domain, as predicted by the nonlinear Schrödinger equation.
elements.sent-2411-301: These results highlight the exceptional capabilities of the P-CNN framework in enabling precise pulse manipulation across spectral and temporal domains.
elements.sent-2411-302: Traditionally, pulse shaping works at very limited spectral bandwidth.
elements.sent-2411-303: The P-CNN boosted nonlinear pulse shaper extends the broad spectrum band, opening new possibilities for targeted spectro-temporal engineering.
elements.sent-2411-304: To extend the applicability of this approach to higher-energy regimes (e.g., $\mu$J-mJ level), integration with Hollow-Core Fibers (HCFs) or other large-mode-area nonlinear structures would be required—while still preserving the spectro-temporal control enabled by the proposed P-CNN-driven shaping method.
elements.sent-2411-305: An exciting avenue for future exploration is to combine spectro-temporal $(\omega,t)$ engineering with spatial degrees of freedom $(k,x)$ to create fully customized space-time designer wavepackets \cite{Jolly2020JO,Korman2022OL,chong2020generation .}
elements.sent-2411-306: This approach can be generalized to manipulate wavepackets in multidimensional correlation spaces, such as $(\omega, k)$ or $(t, x)$, offering transformative potential for nonlinear interactions and advanced beam shaping.
elements.sent-2411-307: Beyond the demonstrations of GNLSE, the P-CNN framework presents a promising machine learning tool for solving other PDEs, which are fundamental in describing natural physical laws \cite{brunton2024promising .}
elements.sent-2411-308: Additionally, this approach has potential applications in accelerating optical computations in emerging fields such as AI photonics \cite{genty2021machine , analog computing \cite{bandyopadhyay2024single , and optical neuromorphic systems \cite{mcmahon2023physics,fischer2023neuromorphic .}}}
elements.sent-2411-309: Finally, the integration of physics-based insights into network design enhances model stability and convergence, particularly for large-scale data training systems.
elements.sent-2411-310: This robustness may help mitigate issues such as "model collapse" in large-language models, paving the way for more reliable and scalable AI-driven physical simulations \cite{shumailov2024ai .}
elements.subsection-2411-001: Methods- 1: The setup and theoretical simulations for supercontinuum and few-cycle pulse
elements.sent-2411-311: Fig. \ref{Fig-setup illustrates the experimental setup for generating supercontinuum (SC) and few-cycle pulses.}
elements.sent-2411-312: The setup comprises five main parts: (a) a homemade mode-locked fiber laser (MLF), (b) the first Erbium-Doped Fiber Amplifier (EDFA-1), (c) a 4f pulse shaper, (d) the second Erbium-Doped Fiber Amplifier (EDFA-2), and (e) a highly nonlinear fiber (HNF) used for SC emission.
elements.sent-2411-313: The MLF is utilized to generate an infrared self-similar soliton pulse, essential for establishing self-similar amplification in both the temporal and spectral domains \cite{ilday2004self .}
elements.sent-2411-314: To achieve this, it is necessary to suppress the Kelly sidebands by optimizing the net dispersion within the cavity, adjusting the pump power in EDFA-0, and finely controlling the polarization by the polarization controller (PC) \cite{Liu2022SM .}
elements.sent-2411-315: The soliton pulse from the MLF is measured using an efficient collinear Frequency-Resolved Optical Gating (FROG) system due to its weak pulse energy \cite{liu2021efficient .}
elements.sent-2411-316: The reconstructed pulse width is approximately 625 fs (see Supplementary S5 for details).
elements.sent-2411-317: Initially, this soliton pulse is stretched and amplified by the EDFA-1, as depicted in Fig \ref{Fig-setup (b).}
elements.sent-2411-318: The amplified soliton pulse is directed through a 4f pulse shaper, as depicted in Fig. \ref{Fig-setup (c).}
elements.sent-2411-319: The pulse shaper assembly comprises two optical gratings, an infrared Spatial Light Modulator (SLM), and two Fourier lenses.
elements.sent-2411-320: After modulation by the 4f pulse shaper, the field can be mathematically represented by the following transfer equation: \begin{equation}\label{E-M2} A_2(t)=\mathcal{F}^{-1}[\mathcal{F}[A_1(t)] \cdot \exp{[i \tilde{\Phi}(\omega)]}]\end{equation} Within the pulse shaper, one can intricately engineer the complex profile of \(\tilde{\Phi (\omega)\), where the real part modulates the spectral phase, and the imaginary part manipulates the spectral amplitude \cite{walmsley2009characterization,bolduc2013exact,liu2019classical,Liu2022SM .}}
elements.sent-2411-321: This precise control enables the fine-tuning of the pulse's spectral-temporal characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-322: The following EDFA-2, as depicted in Fig. \ref{Fig-setup (d), is positioned at the output of the pulse shaper to enhance the pulse energy further.}
elements.sent-2411-323: The dynamics of this amplification can be modeled by the following Nonlinear Schrödinger Equation: \begin{equation}\label{E-M1} i\frac{\partial}{\partial z} A = \frac{\beta_2}{2} \frac{\partial^2}{\partial t^2} A + i\frac{g(z)}{2} A - \gamma |A|^2 A\end{equation} In this equation, the first term on the right side represents the Group Velocity Dispersion (GVD); the second term, \(g(z)\), accounts for the propagation-dependent gain profile; and the last term denotes the Kerr nonlinearity.
elements.formula-2411-014: \begin{equation}\label{E-M1} i\frac{\partial}{\partial z} A = \frac{\beta_2}{2} \frac{\partial^2}{\partial t^2} A + i\frac{g(z)}{2} A - \gamma |A|^2 A\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-324: This formula is also applicable to a single-mode fiber (SMF) by converting the gain term \(g(z)\) to a loss term, which we set to zero due to the short fiber length.
elements.sent-2411-325: The pulse energy is amplified, subsequently inducing a Self-Phase Modulation (SPM) effect because of the Kerr nonlinearity.
elements.sent-2411-326: The resulting pulse field envelope is denoted as \(A_3\) after traversing EDFA-2.
elements.figure-2411-010: \includegraphics[width=17 cm]{Fig_6.pdf} \caption{Setup and Simulations for Supercontinuum and Few-cycle pulse Generation. (a) Mode-locked fiber laser (MLF): The core laser source for generating initial soliton pulses. (b) EDFA-1: Includes an Erbium-doped fiber (EDF) \(L_0\) of 1.1 m, and single-mode fiber (SMF) $\sim$ 2.6 m. (c) Pulse Shaper: Comprises two symmetric optical gratings (940 grooves /\ mm) and lenses (focal length $\sim$ 150 mm) positioned on either side of a Spatial Light Modulator (SLM, Santec SLM-100: 1440 $\times $1050 pixels). (d) EDFA-2: Consists of a forward SMF \(L_1\) of 1.2 m, an Er-doped fiber \(L_2\) of 2 m, and an attached SMF \(L_3\) of 0.8 m. (e) Supercontinuum generation: utilized with a Highly Nonlinear Fiber (HNF) of the length \(L_4\) of $\sim$ 4 cm. (f) and (g) Dynamics of the Pulse and Spectral Intensity in simulations: Traces the evolution of the pulse and its spectral intensity through SMF (\(L_1\)), EDFA-2 (\(L_2\)), and SMF (\(L_3\)). (h) Temporal Intensity Profile: Displays the temporal intensity at the position where SMF \(L_3\) is approximately 0.8 m, with the resultant pulse width around 46 fs. (i) Calculated SC Spectrum: Shows the supercontinuum from 1 to 2.5 $\mathrm{\mu m}$ at a propagation length of 3.5 cm. (j) and (k) Dynamics of SC: Depicts the spectral and temporal intensity variations of the SC along the HNF from 0 to 5 cm.}\label{Fig-setup}

elements.sent-2411-344: Fig.~\ref{Fig-CNN-1 (a) illustrates the structure of the physics-involved convolutional neural network (CNN) utilized for the regression task in supercontinuum (SC) generation, with the detailed operations of each layer shown in Fig.~\ref{Fig-CNN-1 (b).}}
elements.sent-2411-345: The input pulse (before the shaper) is reconstructed from FROG measurements, providing the initial spectral amplitude and phase.
elements.sent-2411-346: By combining the initial field with the setting spectral phase obtained from $\{X_n\}$, we numerically compute the corresponding temporal field.
elements.sent-2411-347: The Wigner distribution is then calculated from this field using Eq.~\ref{E-3 .}
elements.sent-2411-348: This approach leverages the fact that the pulse shaper exhibits a reliable response between the programmed phase and the output field, as experimentally validated in our previous work~\cite{liu2023experimental .}
elements.sent-2411-349: This assumption allows for efficient dataset generation without requiring full experimental characterization for every instance.
elements.sent-2411-350: The resulting Wigner function is cropped and downsampled from $8192 \times 8192$ to $241 \times 81$ pixels before being used as the input to the CNN.
elements.sent-2411-351: The first operation is a two-dimensional convolution layer, employing 32 filters of size $3\times3$, which are applied to the input image to extract features by emphasizing spatial hierarchies.
elements.sent-2411-352: Following this, the Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU) layer acts as the second layer.
elements.sent-2411-353: It outputs the input directly if it is positive; otherwise, it produces zero.
elements.sent-2411-354: This activation function introduces non-linearity into the model, enabling the network to learn complex patterns and improve prediction accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-355: Subsequently, a `maxPooling' layer with a $2\times 2$ pool size is incorporated to reduce the spatial dimensions of the input feature maps.
elements.sent-2411-356: This reduction decreases both the computational complexity and the number of parameters within the network.
elements.sent-2411-357: By maintaining a consistent layer structure to perform similar operations—convolution, ReLU activation, and max pooling, the network could capture more deep features from the input.
elements.sent-2411-358: Following this, a flatten layer is employed to transform multi-dimensional inputs into a one-dimensional vector.
elements.sent-2411-359: This transformation is essential for this application, as the output is a one-dimensional spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-360: Subsequently, three `fullyConnectedLayer' s are utilized to gradually reduce the data dimensionality from $3N_{spectrum}$ to $N_{spectrum}$, where $N_{spectrum}$ represents the sampling number of the spectrum, noted as 436 for the Net-S.
elements.sent-2411-361: The architecture culminates with a `regressionLayer', which directly connects the target spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-362: This layer finalizes the predictive model by linking the learned features to the desired continuous outcome, facilitating accurate spectral regression based on the input characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-363: All these functions in CNN are assistant built-in Matlab deep learning toolbox.

elements.sent-2411-365: This extensive dataset was processed using the `Adam' optimizer to train the network, in which the involved 75\% for training, 15\% for validation, and 10\% for testing.
